worth_it_explanation: DoubleVerify (DV) Pinnacle is where measurement profiles, suitability settings, and reporting configurations live. A user who can alter those settings can change what DV flags as brand-unsafe, how viewability is counted, or how fraud thresholds are applied, all of which flow directly into campaign optimization decisions. These checks are fast to complete inside the Pinnacle account settings, the fixes are a role change or a user removal, and the resulting access model reflects who on the team actually needs to configure DV versus who only needs to read its reports.
intro: DV Pinnacle uses a role-based access model. Users with configuration-level access can change measurement profiles, suitability settings, and verification parameters. Users with reporting-level access can view dashboards and pull data without altering how measurement is applied. The distinction matters because a change to a suitability profile or a fraud threshold affects every campaign that profile is attached to. These checks confirm that the access levels in Pinnacle reflect what each user is actually responsible for, that no accounts belonging to departed staff or former agency contacts remain active, and that reporting-only users cannot alter the configuration settings that govern how DV measures and reports.

audit_checks.title: Users hold configuration-level access when their work is limited to reporting
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A user with configuration-level access in DV Pinnacle can modify measurement profiles, suitability segments, and fraud thresholds. Assigning that level of access to analysts or agency contacts whose work is limited to reading reports creates an unnecessary surface for accidental or unauthorized changes to settings that govern how verification is applied across active campaigns.

educate.base:
DV Pinnacle separates users by what they can do in the platform. Users with configuration-level access can create and modify measurement profiles, adjust suitability categories, set fraud avoidance thresholds, and change how verification is applied to campaigns or line items. Users with reporting-level access can view dashboards, pull data, and export reports without touching any of those settings.

When configuration-level access is assigned broadly rather than only to users whose jobs require it, any of those users can alter a setting that affects campaigns they are not responsible for. A suitability profile change that unintentionally widens the category exclusions, or a threshold adjustment that changes how a fraud signal is weighted, takes effect across every campaign the profile is attached to. The person who made the change may not be aware of the downstream impact because their day-to-day work is in reporting, not configuration.

The appropriate model is to assign configuration-level access only to users who are responsible for setting up and maintaining DV measurement profiles, and to assign reporting-level access to analysts, client-side contacts, and anyone else whose job is to read the data rather than change how it is produced.

A few patterns suggest access may be broader than necessary:
  • Analysts who build decks from DV Pinnacle data hold access that also lets them modify the suitability or fraud settings behind that data
  • Agency reporting contacts hold configuration-level access because it was faster to provision than a more limited role
  • Multiple users on the account hold the highest available access level even though only one or two are responsible for measurement configuration
You may want to review the user list in Pinnacle account settings and compare each person's access level against their actual responsibilities before making adjustments.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, the analyst or media buyer reviewing DV data alongside conversion performance typically needs reporting access only. You may want to confirm that ecommerce-side contacts who pull viewability or fraud data for weekly pacing reports do not also hold access that lets them modify the measurement profiles those reports draw from.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a client-side contact reviewing DV brand-safety data for compliance purposes typically needs reporting access. You may want to confirm that contacts reviewing suitability reports do not hold access that lets them alter the suitability categories the reports are built on.

educate.learn_more_note: DoubleVerify Pinnacle user-management documentation is accessible to credentialed account holders through the Pinnacle platform settings. Contact your DoubleVerify account representative for role configuration guidance specific to your account tier.

investigate.intro: Review the user list in DV Pinnacle account settings and compare each user's access level against their day-to-day responsibilities in the platform.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: Log in to DV Pinnacle and navigate to the account or organization settings. Locate the user management section, which typically lists each user alongside their assigned access level.
For each user with configuration-level access (the level that allows modifying measurement profiles, suitability settings, or fraud thresholds), note their name, email, and role.

For each configuration-level user, confirm whether their actual work involves creating or modifying DV measurement settings. A user whose job is pulling reports or reviewing dashboards is a candidate to move to a reporting-only access level.

Note any configuration-level users who are agency contacts or third-party vendors rather than members of the team directly responsible for DV setup. Agency reporting contacts typically need reporting access, not configuration access.
Document each mismatch: user name, current access level, actual job function, and the access level their job requires.

evaluate.logic: For each user flagged during the review, record their name, current access level, and whether that level matches their actual responsibilities. A user whose access level matches their job function passes. A user whose access level grants configuration rights they do not use or need is a fail, recorded with the user's name, current access level, and the access level their work requires.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each user's name, current access level, actual job responsibilities, and whether the access level is appropriate to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s DV Pinnacle account includes users holding configuration-level access whose day-to-day work is limited to reviewing reports and dashboards. Configuration-level access in Pinnacle allows modifying measurement profiles, suitability settings, and fraud thresholds, any of which can alter how verification is applied across active campaigns. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the user list against each person's actual responsibilities and moving reporting-only users to the appropriate reporting access level, reserving configuration-level access for the team members who set up and maintain DV measurement profiles.

audit_checks.title: Departed staff or former agency contacts still hold active DV Pinnacle access
audit_checks.impact_rationale: DV Pinnacle does not expire or remove user access automatically when an employment relationship or agency engagement ends. A former team member or agency contact who held configuration-level access retains the ability to view measurement data, modify suitability profiles, and change verification settings for as long as their account remains active. The account can be used after the relationship ends without the current team being aware.

educate.base:
DV Pinnacle user access is managed manually. There is no automatic deprovisioning tied to an employment end date, a contract expiration, or a change in agency roster. A user account stays active at whatever access level it was last set to until an administrator explicitly removes it.

A former employee who held configuration-level access in Pinnacle retains the ability to view campaign measurement data and, if the account is not removed, to modify verification settings that govern active campaigns. A former agency contact who managed DV profiles retains visibility into measurement data, suitability configurations, and reporting that may include sensitive campaign or budget information. Neither of these situations requires deliberate misuse to be a problem: a login from a former employee's unrevoked credentials, or a compromised account at a former agency, can produce changes that are difficult to trace after the fact.

Stale access arises in predictable ways. An offboarding process that covers corporate email, VPN, and CRM access but does not include a DV Pinnacle step leaves the access open. An agency transition that removes a contact from internal systems does not propagate to third-party platforms unless someone explicitly completes that step. A vendor relationship that wound down six months ago may still appear as an active user in the Pinnacle account.

A few patterns point to stale access in the user list:
  • User accounts associated with email addresses at agencies or vendors the client no longer works with
  • User accounts for email addresses that are no longer active in the client's identity provider or corporate directory
  • Users who have not logged in for an extended period but still hold configuration-level access
You may want to cross-reference the DV Pinnacle user list against the client's current roster of active employees and active agency or vendor relationships before removing anyone. A user who appears inactive may still hold a legitimate role.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, a former agency contact who ran past display or video campaigns may still hold access to DV measurement data that includes conversion-level performance information. You may want to confirm that access for past agency teams was removed when the relationship ended and not only when the campaign was paused.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a former contact with configuration-level access can still view DV suitability and fraud data associated with lead-generation campaigns. You may want to confirm that contacts from ended engagements were removed from Pinnacle and not only from the media buying platform.

educate.learn_more_note: DoubleVerify Pinnacle user-management documentation is accessible to credentialed account holders through the Pinnacle platform settings. Contact your DoubleVerify account representative for guidance on user deprovisioning and offboarding procedures.

investigate.intro: Pull the full user list in DV Pinnacle account settings and cross-reference it against the client's current roster of active employees, active agency contacts, and active vendor relationships.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: Log in to DV Pinnacle and navigate to the account or organization settings. Open the user management section and export or note the full list of active users, including each user's name, email address, and access level.

Review each email domain in the user list against the client's current agency roster and vendor relationships. Flag any email domain that belongs to an agency or vendor the client no longer works with.
For internal users, cross-reference the email addresses against the client's current corporate directory or identity provider. Flag any address that is no longer active.

For each flagged user, confirm with the client's account owner whether the person still has a legitimate reason to hold DV Pinnacle access. Remove or deactivate accounts the client confirms are no longer active.

Note whether DV Pinnacle shows a last-login date or activity timestamp for each user. Users who have not logged in for an extended period and whose access level is configuration-level are higher-priority candidates for review.

evaluate.logic: For each flagged user, record their name, email domain, access level, and whether the client confirms they are still active. A user confirmed as currently active with a legitimate role passes. A user the client cannot confirm as active, or whose email domain belongs to a former agency or vendor, is a fail, recorded with the user's name, email, access level, and the reason for the flag.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each flagged user's name, email, access level, and active-or-departed status to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} user accounts in {{site_name}}'s DV Pinnacle that belong to contacts who are no longer part of the active account team or whose continued association with the account cannot be confirmed. These accounts retain the access level assigned when the relationship was active, which in some cases includes configuration-level access to measurement profiles and suitability settings. {{voice_recommend}} cross-referencing the full Pinnacle user list against the client's current employee and agency roster, removing any account the client confirms is no longer active, and adding a DV Pinnacle access review to the standard offboarding checklist for employees and agency transitions.

audit_checks.title: No separation between users who configure measurement and users who report on it
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When configuration-capable and reporting-only users are not separated in DV Pinnacle, anyone who can view measurement data can also change the parameters behind it. A person reviewing brand-safety or viewability results can, whether intentionally or not, alter a suitability profile, adjust a fraud threshold, or modify the measurement settings that produced the data they are reviewing. This collapses the control that would otherwise require two separate parties to both approve a configuration change.

educate.base:
In a well-structured DV Pinnacle account, the users who configure measurement and suitability settings are not the same users who review and report on those settings' outputs. This separation serves a practical purpose: it means that a change to a DV configuration requires deliberate action by someone who is responsible for DV setup, and it prevents a reporting-side user from inadvertently altering the parameters behind data they are analyzing.

DV Pinnacle settings that can affect measurement outputs include: measurement profile assignments (which determine what signals DV applies to a campaign), suitability category selections (which define what content is flagged as brand-unsafe), fraud avoidance thresholds (which govern how aggressive DV's fraud filtering is), and viewability measurement parameters. A change to any of these propagates immediately to the campaigns the profile is attached to and affects the data that reporting users then see.

When no separation exists and all users hold configuration-level access, several problems become harder to manage. It is difficult to audit who changed a setting and whether that person was authorized to do so. A reporting-side analyst who makes an exploratory configuration change to test a hypothesis alters live measurement for campaigns they may not be responsible for. A client-side contact reviewing performance data can change the DV settings governing that data in the same session.

A few signs suggest that configuration and reporting access are not separated:
  • All or most users in Pinnacle hold the same access level, with no distinction between those who set up DV and those who use its reports
  • The DV account has no defined group of users who are explicitly limited to read or reporting access
  • Configuration changes to measurement profiles or suitability settings cannot be attributed to a specific user or role in any available audit log
You may want to identify which users are responsible for DV configuration decisions and which users consume DV data in a reporting capacity, then confirm that the access levels in Pinnacle reflect that distinction.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, the analyst who reviews DV viewability data alongside ROAS (return on ad spend) to evaluate media quality is performing a reporting function. You may want to confirm that this analyst does not also hold access that lets them modify the viewability measurement profile the ROAS analysis depends on. A profile change mid-flight would alter the measurement baseline the ROAS analysis draws from.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, the team member who reviews DV brand-safety flags to assess whether suitability settings are too restrictive is performing an analytical function. You may want to confirm that any resulting adjustment to the suitability settings is made by the person responsible for DV configuration rather than the analyst who surfaced the question.

educate.learn_more_note: DoubleVerify Pinnacle user-management documentation is accessible to credentialed account holders through the Pinnacle platform settings. Contact your DoubleVerify account representative for guidance on structuring access levels to separate configuration and reporting functions.

investigate.intro: Review the user list in DV Pinnacle account settings to determine whether users are differentiated by access level, and whether that differentiation maps to the distinction between configuration responsibilities and reporting responsibilities.

dv_pinnacle.label: DoubleVerify Pinnacle
steps: Log in to DV Pinnacle and navigate to the account or organization settings. Open the user management section and note the access level assigned to each user.

Identify users who are responsible for DV configuration decisions: setting up measurement profiles, assigning profiles to campaigns, adjusting suitability categories, and changing fraud thresholds. These users should hold configuration-level access.
Identify users whose work is limited to reviewing DV dashboards, pulling reports, or analyzing data for campaign optimization. These users should hold reporting-level access.
Compare the two lists against the actual access levels in Pinnacle. Flag any user in the reporting group who holds configuration-level access.

If DV Pinnacle provides an activity or change log, review it for configuration changes made by users whose role is primarily reporting-oriented. Any such change is a signal that the separation is not functioning as intended.

evaluate.logic: For each user, record whether their access level matches their functional role: configuration or reporting. A user with configuration-level access who is responsible for DV setup passes. A user with configuration-level access whose function is reporting and who is not responsible for DV configuration is a fail, recorded with the user's name, current access level, and their actual function. An account where all users hold the same access level with no reporting-only tier is a fail at the account level.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each user's name, access level, and functional role in the account to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s DV Pinnacle account does not separate users by configuration access and reporting access: all or most users hold a level of access that lets them both review measurement data and modify the settings behind it. This means that anyone reviewing a brand-safety or viewability report can also change the measurement profile or suitability settings that produced the data. {{voice_recommend}} identifying which users are responsible for DV configuration decisions and assigning reporting-level access to all others, so that changes to measurement profiles, suitability categories, and fraud thresholds require action by a designated configuration owner rather than being available to any user who logs in to pull a report.
